planting.note: Plant certified seed pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 plant January–February for May harvest before summer heat. Yukon Gold is a mid-season variety (70–90 days) well-suited to the Gulf Coast's cool winter growing window. Cut seed pieces to egg size, each with 1–2 eyes; allow cut surfaces to cure 1–2 days before planting.

directions: Plant certified seed pieces 4 inches deep in loose, well-amended soil in full sun; Yukon Gold is a Canadian-bred variety introduced in 1980 that transformed the North American potato market by demonstrating that consumers would pay a premium for a potato with inherently golden flesh and superior flavor. The medium-large, round to oval tubers have smooth, thin, slightly golden skin and deep butter-yellow flesh with a naturally buttery, slightly sweet flavor that requires no added butter to taste rich and satisfying. Yukon Gold is equally excellent baked, mashed, roasted, and sautéed — its lower starch content than russets makes it a smooth masher that doesn't become gluey. It is now the most widely planted 'specialty' potato in North America and has spawned an entire category of yellow-flesh varieties. In zone 9, plant January–February in loose, well-drained soil; hill soil around stems as they emerge. Harvest new potatoes when plants flower (60–70 days); for cured storage, harvest after vines die back. Cure at 60–70°F for one week before storing in a cool, dark location.
sources: Agriculture Canada Research Station, Guelph — Yukon Gold potato release notes 1980
Seed Savers Exchange — Yukon Gold documentation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Potato Production for Texas Home Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Potato Production in Florida

planting.note: Plant certified seed pieces January–February in zone 9 for May–June harvest; Red LaSoda is specifically adapted to the Gulf South's winter planting schedule and performs better in warm, humid climates than most northern potato varieties. It tolerates slightly warmer soil temperatures than white potatoes and handles the transition into warm spring weather better.

directions: Plant certified seed pieces 4 inches deep in well-drained, loose soil; Red LaSoda is a red-skin, white-flesh potato variety developed at Louisiana State University specifically for Gulf Coast and Southern growing conditions — it is one of the few potato varieties bred with the hot, humid Deep South in mind rather than the cool Pacific Northwest or Northeast where most commercial varieties originate. The round, attractively smooth, deep-red skinned tubers have firm, white, waxy flesh excellent for boiling, potato salads, and roasting — the waxy texture holds shape during cooking rather than falling apart. Red LaSoda shows better tolerance of warm temperatures, clay soils, and the wet winters common to the Gulf South than most commercial varieties. It is the most widely recommended red potato for Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and Texas Gulf Coast gardens, and is a staple offering at Southern feed-and-seed stores each January. Harvest as new potatoes when plants bloom; for storage potatoes, harvest after vines begin yellowing and dying back.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Red LaSoda potato development
LSU AgCenter Publication — Potato Production in Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Potato Variety Recommendations for Texas
Mississippi State University Extension — Home Garden Potato Production

planting.note: Plant certified seed pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 plant January–February. Kennebec is a mid-season white potato (75–90 days) that adapts well to the Gulf Coast's winter growing window. Plant 4 inches deep in loose, well-drained soil and hill regularly.

directions: Plant certified seed pieces 4 inches deep in loose, well-drained, fertile soil; Kennebec is an All-America Selections winner introduced by the USDA in 1948 from a cross of B127 x USDA 96-56, named for the Kennebec River in Maine. For many decades it was the most widely grown potato in the eastern United States, dominating both fresh market and potato chip production because of its outstanding adaptability, disease resistance, and excellent culinary quality. The large, oval tubers have thin, buff-white skin and firm, white flesh that performs equally well baked, fried, mashed, or roasted — a true all-purpose potato. Kennebec shows good resistance to late blight and is tolerant of common scab, making it more reliable than many varieties in the variable conditions of Gulf Coast winter gardens. It remains a benchmark variety offered by heritage seed companies for its proven performance and consistent quality. In zone 9, plant January–February and harvest May–June before summer heat arrives.
sources: USDA Bureau of Plant Industry — Kennebec potato release notes 1948
Maine Potato Board — Kennebec variety documentation
Seed Savers Exchange
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Kennebec potato notes

planting.note: Plant whole small tubers or cut large ones with 1–2 eyes in January–February in zone 9; Russian Banana fingerlings are typically planted whole (they are small enough) rather than cut. They mature in 70–90 days and should be harvested before summer heat in zone 9 — target April–May harvest.

directions: Plant whole small tubers or cut pieces 3–4 inches deep in loose, rich soil; Russian Banana is the most widely grown fingerling potato in the United States, an heirloom variety brought from Eastern Europe (likely Latvia or Russia) by immigrants in the early 20th century. The distinctive, curved, 3–4 inch tubers with smooth, pale yellow skin and deep golden-yellow flesh look remarkably like small bananas when harvested and rinsed — hence the name. Flavor is exceptional: buttery, nutty, and intensely potato-tasting with the creamy texture of a waxy potato that holds its shape beautifully when roasted, boiled, or pan-fried with herbs. Russian Banana fingerlings have become the premium specialty potato at farmers' markets and upscale restaurants because of their appearance, flavor, and versatility. Harvest when plants begin to die back; fingerlings store well in a cool, dark location after curing. In zone 9, plant January–February for April–May harvest. The small size makes them quick to cook — roasted whole with olive oil and rosemary, they are outstanding.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Russian Banana Fingerling documentation
Wood Prairie Family Farm — Fingerling potato varieties
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Fingerling Potato variety notes

planting.note: Plant certified seed pieces January–February in zone 9; All Blue is a mid-season variety (75–90 days) that maintains its striking blue-purple flesh color throughout cooking. Plan for May harvest before summer heat arrives in zone 9.

directions: Plant certified seed pieces 4 inches deep in rich, loose, well-drained soil; All Blue (also called All Purple) is a heritage variety with Andean ancestry, producing medium-sized, oblong tubers with deep blue-purple skin and intensely blue-purple flesh throughout — the color does not fade with cooking, making brilliant blue-purple mashed potatoes, blue potato salad, and striking roasted wedges. The color is produced by anthocyanins — the same pigments in blueberries — that are potent antioxidants. Flavor is earthy and slightly nutty, pleasant but not as rich as Yukon Gold or fingerlings. All Blue has relatively firm, waxy flesh that holds its shape when boiled or roasted. The plants are vigorous and productive. In zone 9, the unusual color makes them a conversation-starting crop at the farmers' market or dinner table; they are consistently one of the most visually dramatic vegetables in the garden when cut open. Plant alongside Red LaSoda for a striking red, white, and blue potato planting.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — All Blue potato heritage documentation
Wood Prairie Family Farm — Blue/Purple Potato varieties
Johnny's Selected Seeds — All Blue variety notes
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange
